original_hypothesis.title: Misconfiguration risk: transferAllowedContract may not be set (or set incorrectly), bricking all Arbitrum TITN transfers except to lzEndpoint
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: On Arbitrum (chainid 42161), _validateTransfer restricts all transfers unless sender is owner or either side equals transferAllowedContract, or recipient is lzEndpoint. If transferAllowedContract is unset/incorrect, users receiving TITN from MergeTgt may be unable to move tokens to expected integration(s), causing functional lockups and breaking distribution/claim UX.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The restriction logic hardcodes Arbitrum behavior and relies on an externally set allowlist. The deployment script arbitrumSetup.ts sets transferAllowedContract to MergeTgt, but any deviation (missed step, wrong address, later change) can break transfers for all users.
deployment_assumptions: Admin fails to execute arbitrumSetup.ts step 1, or later updates transferAllowedContract away from required integration
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: arbitrumSetup.ts performs titn.setTransferAllowedContract(MergeTgt). No on-chain enforcement exists to ensure it stays correct.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Arbitrum condition (isBridgedTokenHolder[from] || block.chainid == 42161) combined with isBridgedTokensTransferLocked enforces near-global transfer lock unless transferAllowedContract/lzEndpoint exceptions apply.

preconditions: transferAllowedContract is zero address or not the intended integration

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Global restriction on Arbitrum depends on a single configurable allowlist address
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Incorrect config can freeze transfers for all non-owner accounts
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No sanity checks (e.g., nonzero, contract code size) and no multi-allowlist support

analysis_questions: Is transferAllowedContract expected to remain MergeTgt forever, or rotate to a staking contract post-merge?
What happens to users' ability to move TITN when transferAllowedContract changes?
Does the protocol have documented operational procedures for updating this address safely?
validation_requirements: Simulate Arbitrum with transferAllowedContract unset; attempt transfer between users and confirm revert
Simulate updates to transferAllowedContract and evaluate migration path for existing holders
Review deployment/runbooks to ensure the setter is executed atomically with launch

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: No direct attacker profit required; a configuration mistake (or owner operational error) results in a chain-wide DoS for TITN transfers, impacting all users.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Medium: users’ tokens become illiquid/immobile on-chain; may force bridging or custodial workarounds
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All non-owner TITN holders on Arbitrum
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Yes, if owner can set the correct transferAllowedContract (unless ownership is renounced)

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing integration tests ensuring correct allowlist behavior across lifecycle changes

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Watch TransferAllowedContractUpdated events and verify correct address

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
